results:
3.1. Microsphere morphology and size
Insulin-loaded PLA and PLGA microspheres were successfully prepared using a multiple emulsion-solvent evaporation technique. Particle size determination by laser light diffraction revealed that the PLA microspheres had a volume weighted mean diameter of 41 mm. PLGA microspheres, in contrast, were slightly larger with a mean particle size of 53 mm (Table 1).
Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) pictures of PLA and PLGA microspheres containing 5% bovine insulin are shown in Fig. 1a-d. Microspheres were clearly spherical and covered by small pore openings on their surface. Moreover, the surface of the PLGA microspheres was found to be more porous than that of PLA microspheres. Uchida et al.  attributed the microsphere surface pores to organic solvent that diffused from the particle core to the surface during the particle hardening. The encapsulation efficiency was 75% and 80% for PLA and PLGA, respectively, which could be considered sufficiently high for the subsequent stability studies.
3.2. In vitro release from PLA and PLGA microspheres
The goal of the release investigations was to ensure that insulin was entrapped in the microspheres over a sufficiently long time interval for the protein to interact with the polymer. Release profiles of insulin from PLA and PLGA microspheres are displayed in Fig. 2. Both microsphere types displayed release patterns that would be not favorable for a therapeutical application, but that can be considered ideal for the purpose of investigating polymer protein interactions. During the first 6 h, an initial burst release led to an early loss of 66% and 71% of the protein dose from PLA and PLGA microspheres, respectively, which can at least in part be attributed to the amount of insulin located on or near the microsphere surface , . While many authors report protein retention to be a frequently encountered problem for PLA and PLGA microspheres ,  caused by aggregation and hydrophobic polymer-protein interactions , we considered it ideal for our experiments. A sufficiently high amount of the protein was retained inside the microspheres and allowed for investigation into the stability of insulin over a period of several weeks.
SEM pictures proved that after 18 days, PLA and PLGA insulin-loaded microspheres displayed only a partial retention of their shapes with increased porosity (Fig. 3a and b) due to the erosion of the polymer in the aqueous environment. The amount of insulin residing inside the PLA and PLGA microspheres after 18 days was 30% and 25% of the initial amount, respectively.
3.3. Insulin stability in lactic acid solutions
Prior to investigating insulin stability inside PLA and PLGA microspheres during erosion, insulin was incubated in different lactic acid solutions, which proved to be an ideal model system, as it mimics the conditions that prevail during polymer degradation . Fig. 4a shows a total ion chromatogram (TIC) of native insulin, in which a characteristic peak appeared at a retention time of 16.11 min, the mass of which is 5734 Da (Fig. 4b). For comparison, Fig. 5a displays a TIC of insulin incubated in 10% w/w lactic acid solution for 28 days. It contains a peak for insulin at 15.29 min, corresponding to a molecular mass of 5734 Da (Fig. 5b). Besides the insulin signal, another broad peak appears at a retention time of 15.5 min with a molecular mass of 5735 Da (Fig. 5c), which stems from an insulin deamidation product . Upon further analysis of this peak, molecules with a molar mass of 5806 Da were detected, which can be attributed to insulin acylated by one lactic acid molecule (Fig. 5d). Based on the weak signal, it is evident that the prevalence of the monoacylated insulin is very low compared to that of deamidated insulin.
When we incubated insulin in 1%, 5% and 20% w/w lactic acid solutions, we obtained similar results. The prevalence of monoacylated insulin seemed, thereby, to be a function of the lactic acid concentration (data not shown).
3.4. Insulin integrity inside PLA and PLGA microspheres during erosion
After 18 days of erosion, insulin was extracted from PLA microspheres and was examined for its integrity again using HPLC-MS analysis. While insulin was found unaltered prior to erosion (data not shown), TICs recorded after 18 days of erosion revealed the presence of both native insulin and degradation products at retention times of 15.66 and 15.96 min, respectively (Fig. 6a). Mass spectra recorded at these retention times confirmed the presence of insulin with a molar mass of 5734 Da (Fig 6b) and desamido insulin with a molar mass of 5735 Da (Fig. 6c). Similar results were obtained when insulin was extracted from PLGA microspheres after 18 days (Fig. 7a-c). To our surprise, we did not find any signals that would be indicative of an acylation with lactate or glycolide units.
To follow the kinetics of the appearance of the main degradation product, we extracted microsphere samples at various time points up to 18 days of degradation. The relative area of peaks that stem from insulin and desamido insulin extracted from microsphere samples after 6, 12 and 18 days are displayed in Fig. 8a and b. It is obvious that significant amounts of protein undergo deamidation under these conditions.
discussion:
When bovine insulin was extracted from PLA and PLGA microspheres after 18 days of erosion, HPLC-MS analysis showed that a considerable amount of protein had undergone structural changes. Changes in insulin molecular mass indicate that the main degradation product is desamido insulin. Surprisingly, an acylation with monomers, as has been reported for many peptides, could not be verified, despite an acylation in lactic acid solutions.
Brange et al.  showed that insulin can decompose via a multitude of different chemical reactions. It may deamidate along two main reaction pathways by entirely different mechanisms. In acid media, deamidation of asparagine in position 21 of the A-chain catalyzed intramolecularly by the protonated C-terminal seems to be the prevailing mechanism. This deamidation of bovine insulin in PLGA microspheres has already been extensively reported in the literature . It seems to be spurred by the acidic conditions that prevail during PLA and PLGA degradation. For porcine insulin, in contrast, the two major instability mechanisms were again Asn A21 deamidation and dimer formation . Similar to the findings in this study Shao et al.  reported slow and incomplete porcine insulin release from PLA and PLGA microspheres. It was reported that only about 11% of the insulin extracted from microspheres after release over a period of 4 weeks was intact. The degradation products observed consisted of about 15% deamidated Asn A21 insulin, about 22% covalent insulin dimer and trace amounts of high molecular weight transformation products. Above pH 6, an intermediate imide formation occurs at residue Asn B3, leading to the formation of iso Asp and Asp derivatives.
While the presence of a deamidation product was of little surprise to us, we were amazed that no acylation of insulin occurred during the erosion of PLA or PLGA micropsheres. Its reaction with oligomers in lactic acid solution shows, however, that it is in general prone to this instability mechanism. Na et al.  as well as Lucke et al.  showed that the primary amines of the N-teminal or in the lysine side chains are targets for peptide acylation by lactide and glycolide oligomers. Insulin contains three such potential acylation sites: two N-terminal amino acids (PheB1 and GlyA1) and one lysine side chain (LysB29). Strickley and Anderson  showed that in solution at pH 2.5 and 35 degC, insulin degrades by deamidation and covalent dimerization at the C-terminal AsnA21. This mechanism is initiated by an intramolecular nucleophilic attack of the protonated carboxylic acid of the C-terminal on a side chain amide, forming a reactive cyclic anhydride intermediate. It has been described that this intermediate reacts with water to form desamidoA21 insulin. Alternatively, it can bind to the PheB1 N-terminal amine groups of PheB1 and GlyA1 of other insulin molecules to form desamidoA21-PheB1 or desamidoA21-GlyA1, respectively, whereby the latter reaction is slower than the first one. Reactions with the lysine side chain have not yet been reported.
The selectivity of the reaction with the three nucleophiles can be explained on the basis of the pKa values of their individual amino groups (PheB1 < 7.0, GlyA1 8.0 and LysB29 > 9.0). The amine with the lowest pKa, PheB1, is likely to be the least protonated one inside the acidic microenvironment of the microparticles. Therefore, it will react faster than its two 'competitors'. This is supported by the fact that the lysine side chain with its high pKa is not involved at all in the formation of dimers.
The deamidation mechanism may also explain why we see no acylation products during microsphere erosion. Both cyclic intermediates of insulin and of PLA and PLGA oligomers compete for the same nucleophiles during the formation of covalent dimers and during acylation. Under the conditions that prevail inside the microspheres, the reactive cyclic anhydride inside insulin most likely has a higher reactivity than the five-membered ring oligomer intermediate that was shown to form at the carboxy terminal of PLA and PLGA oligomers. Therefore, by virtue of kinetic reaction control, the deamidation and dimerization may be favored. In addition, insulin exists as monomers in low concentrations only (< 0.1 mM 0.6 mg/ml). At higher concentrations (< 0.01 mM) and low pH values, insulin dimerizes covalently, as described above, and non-covalently. The data obtained by Kaplan et al.  indicated that at least the B29 Lys amino group of insulin is involved in this process and is, therefore, not available for an acylation reaction. Moreover, in contrast to the small peptides, the complex secondary and tertiary structures of insulin may provide a sterically hindered environment and thus prevent the attack of any available free amino groups by the acylating agent.
In the control experiments with lactic acid solutions, the slower reaction of the intermediate seems to be compensated by the higher oligomer concentration. Furthermore, insulin cannot escape acylation by adsorption due to the missing polymer surface.
conclusion: In contrast to many small molecular weight peptides, insulin is mainly destabilized by deamidation rather than by acylation during the erosion of PLA and PLGA polymers. The different reactivities of the activated intermediate cyclic oligomers and of cyclic insulin could account for the phenomenon. Although it cannot be excluded that other factors such as the structure of insulin contribute to the prevention of acylation, one can draw the conclusion that in addition to the presence of free amine groups, the properties of a protein decide to a large degree whether it undergoes acylation or not.
materials_methods:
Poly(d,l-lactic acid) with equal amounts of d- and l-lactic acid (PLA, Mw 17,000, Resomer R 202) and Poly(d,l-lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA, Mw 14,100, Resomer RG 502) with a lactide/glycolide ratio of 50:50, both end-capped by an ethylester group, were obtained from Boehringer Ingelheim (Ingelheim, Germany). Bovine insulin (51 amino acids, Mw 5734 Da) was kindly supplied by Aventis (Frankfurt, Germany). Concentrated lactic acid, analytical grade (approximately 90%), was purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Poly(vinyl alcohol) PVA, 98% hydrolyzed (Av. Mw 13,000-23,000) was obtained from Aldrich Chemical Co. (Milwaukee, USA), 4,4--Dicarboxy-2-2--biquinoline (Bicinchoninic acid, BCA) from Sigma Chemical Co. (St. Louis, USA). Acetonitrile was obtained from Baker (Deventer, The Netherlands). Triflouroacetic acid (TFA) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Seelze, Germany). All other reagents were of analytical grade or higher purity. Water used throughout the investigation was double-distilled and filtered through a cellulose nitrate filter with pores of 0.2 mm diameter (Sartorius, Gottingen, Germany).
2.2. Stability of insulin in lactic acid solutions
Insulin was dissolved in different lactic acid solutions (1%, 5%, 10% and 20% w/w) to get insulin concentrations of 170, 210, 110 and 250 mg/ml, respectively. The resulting solutions (10 ml) were stored in 15 ml polypropylene tubes (Corning Costar, Bodenheim, Germany) at 37 degC for 4 weeks. Samples were drawn on days 7, 14, 21 and 28, frozen in liquid nitrogen and stored at -80 degC (HERAfreeze HFU 586 basic from Kendro, Hanau, Germany) until further analysis. The samples were analyzed using the HPLC-MS method described below.
2.3. Preparation of insulin-loaded microspheres
Insulin-loaded microspheres were prepared by a double emulsion-solvent evaporation technique . In brief, the internal aqueous phase (200 ml of 0.1 N HCl) containing 15 mg insulin was added to the organic phase (300 mg polymer dissolved in 2 ml methylene chloride) under vortex mixing for 30 s and sonication over 40 s in an ice bath using an ultrasound nozzle (250-D Branson Digital Sonifier, Schwabisch Gmund, Germany). 4 ml of a 1% aqueous PVA solution was added to the mixture as an external aqueous phase with 10 s of vortex mixing. The dispersion was then rapidly added to 100 ml of 0.5% aqueous PVA solution and stirred for 2 h to evaporate the solvent. The hardened microspheres were centrifuged, washed twice with double-distilled water, freeze-dried and finally stored at -80 degC.
2.4. Investigation of microsphere loading efficiency
10 mg of freeze-dried microspheres was weighed into a 2 ml micro test tube (Eppendorf, Hamburg, Germany) and dissolved in 600 ml of dichloromethane. 600 ml of a 0.1 N HCl solution were added and the two phases were vortex-mixed (Heidolph, Schwabach, Germany). The dispersion was allowed to settle at room temperature for 10 min before the dichloromethane phase was separated from the mixture by centrifugation at 2000 rpm for 3 min (G-S-15R, Beckman, Palo, Atto, CA, USA). The upper aqueous phase containing the extracted peptide was analyzed using a micro BCA assay and HPLC analysis.
2.5. HPLC and HPLC-MS analysis
Insulin samples were investigated by HPLC using a setup that consisted of a degasser (from Knauer, Berlin, Germany), LC-10-AT pump, FCV-10ATVP gradient mixer, SIL-10 ADVP autosampler, SPD-10 AV UV detector and SCL-10AVP controller (all from Schimadzu, Duisburg, Germany). A linear gradient of 15% to 40% solvent B (90% acetonitrile aqueous solution + 0.1% v/v TFA) in solvent A (10% acetonitrile aqueous solution + 0.1% v/v TFA) over 42 min was applied as mobile phase at a flow rate of 1.0 ml/min. 100-ml samples were separated at room temperature using a combination of a C18 reversed phase pre-column (LC318, 4.6 mm x 50 mm) and analytical column (LC318, 4.6 mm x 250 mm) from Supelco (Deisenhofen, Germany). Chromatograms were recorded at 274 nm (UV detector).
For HPLC-MS analysis, samples were analysed using an Agilent 1100 HPLC system with degasser, binary pump, autosampler, column oven and diode array detector (all from Agilent, Waldbronn, Germany), coupled with a TSQ7000 mass spectrometer (ThermoQuest, San Jose, CA, USA) with API2-source (capillary temperature: 300 degC, spray voltage: 4 kV). A linear gradient of 20-95% solvent B (acetonitrile + 0.1% v/v TFA) in solvent A (double-distilled water + 0.1% v/v TFA) over 30 min served as a mobile phase at a flow rate of 0.2 ml/min. About 10-20 ml of the samples were separated using a C18 reversed phase analytical column (Jupiter, 2 mm x 250 mm) from Phenomenex, Aschaffenburg, Germany. The XCALIBUR(r) software package (ThermoQuest, San Jooee, CA, USA) was used for data acquisition and analysis.
2.6. Microstructure and particle size distribution of insulin-loaded microspheres
The microsphere morphology was investigated after manufacturing and freeze-drying as well as after the release study by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (DSM 950 from Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany). To investigate their internal structure, microspheres were torn between two layers of adhesive tape (Tesa glasklar, Beiersdorf, Hamburg, Germany). All samples were sputter-coated with a 1.4 nm layer of gold/palladium (Paleron SC515 SEM coating system from Fison Surface System, East Grinstead, UK) prior to SEM analysis.
The size distribution of insulin-loaded PLA and PLGA microspheres was investigated using laser light diffraction (Mastersizer 2000, Malvern Instruments, Herrenberg, Germany). For a typical experiment, about 10 mg of microspheres was suspended in 5 ml water and analyzed with an obscuration index (measure of the amount of light lost due to the introduction of the sample into the light path) ranging from 2% to 5%. All samples were analyzed in triplicate. To characterize the size distribution, we assessed the weighted mean of the volume distribution. The sizes below which 10% (d(0.1)), 50% (d(0.5)) and 90% (d(0.9)) of the microspheres fell were especially useful to characterize the microsphere size distribution in more detail.
2.7. Insulin release studies
For the investigation of peptide release from biodegradable polymer microspheres, approximately 10 mg of microspheres was weighed into a 2 ml micro test tube (Eppendorf, Hamburg, Germany) and incubated in 1 ml phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), pH 7.4 (stabilized with 0.02% w/v sodium azide). The samples were eroded under gentle shaking (frequency 10 min-1) at 37 degC in a shaking water bath (Model 1086 from GFL, Burgwerdel, Germany). The buffer was changed and release samples were taken at regular time intervals. All samples were analyzed in triplicate and stored at -80 degC until further analysis.
2.8. Assessment of insulin integrity
The stability of bovine insulin inside polymer microspheres was determined after 6, 12 and 18 days. Microspheres were harvested from the release medium by centrifugation and insulin was extracted using the method described above. The aqueous phase was investigated immediately using HPLC and HPLC-MS analysis.
